topic: Zelenskyy Fires Ukraines Chief Protests Army

left.title: Zelenskyy fires Ukraine’s army chief and offers Fedorov ‘prominent position’

left-center.title: Ukraine’s Zelenskyy fires his commander in chief after protests and major rift

center.title: Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires his military chief after protests and names a new one

farLeftText: Volodymyr Zelenskyy has fired Ukraine’s commander-in-chief Oleksandr Syrskyi and offered a “prominent position” to the defence minister he sacked last week in a move that sparked nationwide protests. In a late-night TV address, Zelenskyy said he was appointing Mykhailo Drapatyi, a popular and innovative general, as the new head of Ukraine’s armed forces. Drapatyi would oversee a reorganisation of the army’s command structure, Ukraine’s president said. Zelenskyy said he was bringing Mykhailo Fedorov back into government after his removal as defence minister prompted thousands of people to demonstrate in Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities, in one of the biggest political crises since Russia’s 2022 invasion. View image in fullscreen Volodymyr Zelenskyy with Oleksandr Syrskyi at the handover of drone missiles to the Ukrainian army in Kyiv in December 2024. Photograph: Sergey Dolzhenko/EPA Ukraine’s president paid tribute to Syrskyi and credited him with leading the defence of Kyiv and launching a counter-invasion of Russia’s Kursk province in summer 2024, in which foreign tanks rolled on to Russian territory for the first time since the second world war. “We have come a long way. The defence of Ukraine continues. And every soldier deserves to be treated with respect,” Zelenskyy said, adding that he was grateful to Syrskyi and to “every one of our soldiers” fighting on the frontline. Zelenskyy will be hoping that his radical reshuffle of the army’s leadership will assuage protesters who have taken to the streets demanding Syrskyi’s removal. They had given a deadline of Friday for Fedorov to be reinstated and promised “indefinite protests” if their request was ignored. 1:32 Why Ukrainians are protesting against Zelenskyy’s dismissal of defence minister – video Fedorov welcomed Drapatyi’s appointment. “This brings a breath of fresh air and new hope to the struggle of free people for freedom and justice. It is a voice for change that could not be ignored,” he said. He added: “The high expectations of the Ukrainian people must be met with a clear vision for ending the war for independence, a strong team and decisive actions aimed at saving the lives of our soldiers, automating the frontline, delivering asymmetric strikes and crippling the Russian economy.” Drapatyi, the new commander-in-chief, is well-regarded within Ukraine’s army and is liked by the public. He is viewed as an ally of Fedorov, whose attempts to reform the military and force it to adopt modern technology and data led to a bitter clash with the old-school Syrskyi. A major general, Drapatyi is commander of Ukraine’s joint forces group. He has been fighting Russia since its covert attempt in 2014 to take over the eastern Donbas region. He became famous nationally when he drove an infantry fighting vehicle over a barricade set up by pro-Russian militants in the city of Mariupol. The Ukrainian media outlet Babel described Drapatyi as one of Ukraine’s most capable battlefield commanders. It said his new job would be fundamentally about reforming a large institution rather than commanding troops in combat. Zelenskyy said he and Drapatyi agreed that Ukraine’s long-range drone attacks on targets in Russia such as oil refineries would continue. “Our programme of precision strikes will be implemented with absolute precision,” he said, promising a smooth and coordinated handover following Syrskyi’s departure. He also said the new commander-in-chief would be tasked with updating the country’s defence strategy and reforming its structure. Another pressing issue is mobilisation, at a time when after nearly five years of full-scale war few Ukrainians are ready to volunteer. It is unclear what Fedorov’s new role will be, amid reports that he was insisting on being given his old job back as defence minister. This has now been filled by Yevhen Khmara, a former special operations commander. View image in fullscreen Yevhen Khmara replaced Mykhailo Fedeorov as Ukraine’s defence minister. Photograph: Ukrainian presidential press service/AFP/Getty Images Zelenskyy said he met Fedorov on Tuesday. “I offered Mykhailo a prominent position within the government that would enable him to unify the technological sector of our state and ensure its development,” he said, without giving further details. Zelenskyy added: “Our shared wish is one – victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace.” He said “a significant path has been travelled, the defence of Ukraine continues, and every warrior deserves dignified treatment”.
centerLeftText: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy fired his commander in chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi, on Tuesday, bowing to pressure after the removal of popular Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov sparked the fiercest leadership crisis of his presidency. Subscribe to read this story ad-free Get unlimited access to ad-free articles and exclusive content. Zelenskyy announced on Telegram that Mykhailo Drapatyi will replace Syrskyi. Zelenskyy’s decision to fire Fedorov, one of his closest allies, led to rare wartime protests on the streets of Kyiv. Fedorov publicly aired his disagreements with Syrskyi, known as “The Butcher,” over military strategy. Syrskyi, 60, is a more traditional military commander focused on infantry and artillery, while Fedorov, 35, is seen as a modernizer, whose focus on technological advancements in drone warfare has led to Ukraine’s recent successes in hitting Russia. Despite the recent shake-up, Zelenskyy tried to present a unified front in his Telegram message Tuesday announcing the dismissal of Syrskyi. “The desire in all of us is one — victory over the enemy and achieving such conditions on the front and in pressure on Russia, which would allow forcing Russia to peace,” he wrote. Worried Ukrainians, fearing that Fedorov’s removal could wipe out recent gains, had taken to the streets calling for him to be reinstated and Syrskyi to be forced out, instead. A senior air force commander also quit in protest. For those in the streets, Syrskyi had become a symbol of the Soviet way of thinking rejected by Ukraine’s younger generations. Fedorov, meanwhile, represents technological innovation and a determination to outsmart the country’s more powerful invader. Syrskyi has been nicknamed “The Butcher” for his unsparing reputation, with critics pointing to lives lost in a late withdrawal from the fortress city of Bakhmut and the disastrous retreat from Russia’s Kursk region last year that left soldiers stranded and forced to walk back for days under enemy fire. Zelenskyy spent the weekend speaking to other top military commanders in an apparent interview process to potentially replace Syrskyi. It is unclear whether Fedorov will remain in the government and, if so, in what capacity. On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country’s technology sector. But it was not clear whether Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets. “Change is inevitable,” Fedorov said Saturday on social media. “The dialogue is happening. I believe we will succeed.” Former Ukrainian Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov holds a briefing in Kyiv on Thursday. Danylo Antoniuk / Future Publishing via Getty Images Zelenskyy did not give a reason for firing Fedorov last week. He said he was effectively left with a choice between the defense minister and Syrskyi, who was heading the armed forces. The two were no longer communicating with each other, he said. Fedorov confirmed his spat with Syrskyi, saying Syrskyi was not willing to discuss “problems” directly and resisted reform of Ukraine’s defense apparatus. 00:22 Protests erupt in Ukraine over defense minister's ouster 00:00 00:00 While he credited Syrskyi for Ukraine’s wins in the early days of the war, “the war has changed” because of drone advancements, Fedorov argued. “Why is this trajectory breaking now, and why are we veering off course? There is such a risk. And the Ukrainian people have taken to the streets for that,” he said at a news conference. Ukraine’s deployment of low-cost drones has upended the battlefield and the traditional model of warfare, with Kyiv demonstrating a growing ability to hit strategic sites deep inside Russia, in addition to critical Russian-held targets within Ukraine’s territory. At the same time, Russian troops continue to slowly advance in eastern Ukraine, while a dwindling supply of missile interceptors has left Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities vulnerable to intense bombardment.
centerText:
Ukraine's Zelenskyy fires his military chief after protests and names a new one

toggle caption Efrem Lukatsky/AP

KYIV, Ukraine (AP) — President Volodymyr Zelenskyy on Tuesday fired Gen. Oleksandr Syrskyi as commander-in-chief of Ukraine's armed forces following days of mass protests in Kyiv and other cities nationwide demanding his removal.

Zelenskyy appointed Mykhailo Drapatyi as the new army chief, according to a statement on social media. "Our shared wish is one — victory over the enemy and conditions at the front and in pressure on Russia that would force Russia toward peace," Zelenskyy said.

The Ukrainian leader said he made the decision after a series of meetings with military commanders. They began shortly after protests broke out demanding the reinstatement of former Defense Minister Mykhailo Fedorov, seen by demonstrators as a driver of military modernization, and the removal of Syrskyi, who they said embodied the army's old, Soviet-style command culture.

Sponsor Message

Zelenskyy thanked Syrskyi for his achievements in Ukraine's fight against Russia and praised him for the defense of Kyiv and for operations in Kharkiv and Kursk. "I am grateful to Oleksandr Syrskyi and to every one of our warriors for Ukraine's strong front-line positions," he said. "I am grateful to Mykhailo Drapatyi for holding that same view."

On Tuesday, Zelenskyy separately met with Fedorov and offered him what he called a respected position in government that would unify the country's technology sector. But it wasn't clear if Fedorov accepted the offer and whether the latest moves would satisfy protesters and bring peace to the streets.

The Trump administration announced Tuesday that it’s halting more than $867 million in federal Medicaid payments to the Golden State over suspected fraud — with one expert describing it as a “piece of cake” for scammers to cash in. “We are not sending Medicaid dollars out the door until we have confidence that they are being spent lawfully and appropriately,” Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said during a press conference Tuesday. 6 Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said the Trump administration is halting over $1 billion in medicaid payments to California and Minnesota. ZUMAPRESS.com The scandal centers around California’s In-Home Supportive Services (IHSS), a Medicaid-funded program that pays caregivers — often a family member — to help elderly and disabled residents at home instead of moving into a facility. Over the past two years, Centers for Medicare & Medicaid Services (CMS) Administrator Mehmet Oz said California’s spending for in-home services was “twice the rate of the average of the rest of the entire nation.” 6 CMS administrator Mehmet Oz said California’s spending for in-home services was “twice the rate of the average of the rest of the entire nation.” REUTERS “If it smells like fraud, we’re not paying for it anymore,” he said, adding that CMS is deferring payments until California can “substantiate their claims from this past quarter audit.” Roughly $199 million in Medicaid funding in Minnesota was also frozen as part of the action. Oz said CMS flagged suspicious billing patterns, including providers who submitted claims more than a year after services were provided, billed four or more patients simultaneously or ranked among the top 2.5% of billers nationwide. Haywood Talcove, CEO of LexisNexis Risk Solutions for Government and a nationally recognized fraud expert, told The California Post that taxpayers should be “outraged” over the alleged lack of oversight that he said enables widespread fraud in California. “Anytime you have a program like this where it is self-reported, there is virtually no auditing, and no one gets caught, then the probability of fraud is very, very high,” he said, estimating that fraud accounts for roughly 20% of the IHSS program. 6 Oz said CMS flagged suspicious billing patterns, including providers who submitted claims more than a year after services were provided. via REUTERS Talcove explained how fraudsters can exploit IHSS, describing the process as a “piece of cake.” According to Talcove, the fraud scheme begins when someone submits an IHSS application claiming to need in-home care, then designates a caregiver and persuades a licensed healthcare professional to falsely certify their need for care in exchange for a “cut.” “I’ll tell you that your fake person that doesn’t exist needs home healthcare, and guess what? You’re going to give me a little cut. That’s how it goes,” Talcove said, adding that the “state doesn’t have the technology, doesn’t have the people, and doesn’t have the systems to validate it.” 6 There are more than 500,000 people enrolled in California’s In-Home Supportive Services (IHSS). Getty Images Talcove argued California lacks the staffing, technology and incentive to adequately verify the state’s more than 500,000 IHSS recipients. “There’s no money that comes out of the state pocket,” he said. “The incentive structure — federally funded, state-administered — is a recipe for disaster.” California Governor Gavin Newsom rejected the administration’s claims of rampant fraud, describing the decision to halt payments as “pure politics.” 6 California Governor Gavin Newsom called Tuesday’s announcement “pure politics.” Carlin Stiehl for CA Post “We take fraud seriously,” Newsom said. “We don’t politicize it like these guys.” The governor claimed that Tuesday’s announcement was an attack on his potential successor Xavier Becerra, the Democratic candidate for governor and a former Health and Human Services chief under former President Biden. Newsom also said his office has been actively cooperating with the Trump administration, pointing to communications in which information requested from California was already provided. “California’s approach, which had been long approved by CMS, saves federal and state taxpayers money,” a spokesperson for Newsom’s office told The Post. “One year of in-home care saves state and federal taxpayers approximately $100,000 per person annually versus nursing facility care. A 2020 California State Auditor report found no program integrity concerns and recommended increasing reimbursement rates to grow the in-home care provider workforce and reduce reliance on costly institutional care.” 6 Kennedy said California and Minnesota will have to provide documentation proving the payments are legitimate if they want to restore funding. REUTERS Talcove believes the only way to prevent future fraud and abuse is to issue block grants and give the money directly to the states, or have the program fully funded and administered at the federal level. “You can’t arrest and prosecute your way out of this,” he said. In May, the Trump administration withheld $1.3 billion in Medicaid funding from California over mounting concerns tied to the state’s hospice and home-health agencies — bringing the current total over $2 billion. The funds will be frozen until California and Minnesota can substantiate their claims and while federal officers review “high-risk” Medicaid claims. “They need to provide documentation that these payments are legitimate,” Kennedy said.
farRightText: The Trump-Kennedy Department of Health and Human Services (HHS) has paused over $1 billion in Medicaid funding for California and Minnesota, as the Democrats there have absolutely no intention of truly reforming their systems to root out the rampant fraud. Advertisement HHS leadership held a press conference Tuesday regarding the suspension and how the Trump administration is combatting fraud. “If those states want that money, they need to provide documentations that these ⁠payments are legitimate," HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. said at the press conference where Dr. Mehmet Oz, administrator of the Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS), also appeared. Oz explained why California in particular is raising so many questions. "Another $250 million, a quarter billion dollars, comes from claims tied to high-risk providers, as I explained earlier, those who are billing more than a year after the service is provided, those billing for four or more patients all at the same time — hard to juggle that many patients." He went on, "And if they ranked the top two and a half percent of all billers, we were worried about them. This is a very conservative approach, by the way. We could have taken a lot more money. But this, we believe, is a very thoughtful, judicious way of moving forward. The final comes to about $5 million, by the way, when you also add some of the other red flag issues." Advertisement 🚩 🚩 🚩@DrOzCMS rattles off the red flags suggesting rampant fraud in California. pic.twitter.com/DIlztUXc5f — HHS Rapid Response (@HHSResponse) July 21, 2026 Read Also: U.S. Imposes 50% Tariffs on Some Canadian Goods Over ‘Discriminatory’ Actions Kennedy, meanwhile, stated, “And we have reason to believe taxpayer money is being misused, especially through fraud. We have a duty to stop the payments, demand answers, and then follow the evidence wherever it leads.” Referring to the Democrats in charge of California and Minnesota, “If Gov. Gavin Newsom or Gov. Tim Walz wants this funding released, all they have to do is provide basic documentation, showing that these services are legitimate and not fraudulent. And that's common sense. The Biden administration abandoned that kind of oversight. Instead of protecting your money, they open the floodgates to theft. They dismantled basic program integrity and oversight.” BREAKING: HHS Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. announced that the Trump administration is pausing more than $1 billion in Medicaid payments to California and Minnesota over suspected fraud and noncompliance, saying the states must provide documentation showing the payments are… pic.twitter.com/5yX329Mpnw — Breaking911 (@Breaking911) July 21, 2026 Advertisement “Even when they suspected fraud, the Biden HHS sent checks anyway, and tried to claw back the money later,” Kennedy stated. “It didn't make sense and it didn't work. The scammers got paid, the taxpayers got stuck with these enormous bills. Under President Trump, HHS is restoring common sense. We are not sending Medicaid and Medicare dollars out the door until we have confidence that they are being spent safely and appropriately.” That ought to be the standard, instead of the exception. left-center.title: Trump Imposes 50% Tariffs On Most Canadian Goods

center.title: Trump imposes 50% tariffs on Canadian goods

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: LOADING ERROR LOADING WASHINGTON (AP) — President Donald Trump on Monday imposed 50% tariffs on most Canadian goods, declaring that Canada has unfairly discriminated against American autos, alcohol and dairy products. The move could unleash a new wave of economic chaos, with risks of higher inflation and further fraying of relations between two nations that had been closely woven together before Trump’s return to the White House. The administration official previewing the action said that Canada was one of the only nations other than China that retaliated against Trump’s previous tariffs and must be held accountable. Advertisement The official insisted on anonymity on a call with reporters to preview the president’s actions and said that Trump signed three proclamations to launch the tariffs under Section 338 of the 1930 Trade Act. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section because they said Trump could use it to destabilize the economy. The new 50% tariffs would exclude energy products, potash, fish and critical minerals, but they would include goods that had previously been protected from import taxes by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA. That 2020 trade pact was not renewed by the U.S., triggering a new set of negotiations that could run until 2036. The White House said in a fact sheet that the tariffs would go into effect in 30 days, meaning there is time for negotiations as Trump has not always followed through on his announced tax hikes on imports. Advertisement Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney said in a statement that his government believes in the “benefits of free and fair trade,” having signed “more than 20 new economic and security partnerships.” He said Canada is prepared to negotiate with the Trump administration. “This trade dispute has raised costs for families, particularly in the U.S.,” Carney said. “Canada stands ready to engage intensively to address outstanding issues with the U.S. to the mutual benefit of our citizens.” U.S. President Donald Trump, left, speaks with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney, right, at a working lunch with leaders of G7 and the Middle East, in Evian-les-Bains, France, on June 16, 2026. Evelyn Hockstein/Pool Photo via AP Advertisement Canada Faces The Risk Of A Broader Trade War Still, the tariffs could escalate into a wider trade war as Canada seeks to defend its economy. Ontario Premier Doug Ford saw a possible showdown ahead. “If these tariffs proceed, Canada should respond tariff for tariff, dollar for dollar,” Ford posted on social media. Candace Laing, CEO of the Canadian Chamber of Commerce, said the Trump administration’s moves were “regrettable” but the two countries need to use the 30-day window before the tariffs start “to make meaningful progress in advancing formal talks.” Advertisement Chris Swonger, CEO of the Distilled Spirits Council of the United States, also called for a deal: “We encourage policymakers on both sides of the border to pursue a negotiated solution that restores market access for U.S. spirits and avoids further harm to the U.S. hospitality sector.” But the use of a Great Depression-era law to impose the tariffs broadens some of the risks, as those tariffs could be applied to other U.S. trading partners, not just Canada, and inject “massive uncertainty” into the global economy, said Scott Lincicome, vice president of general economics at the Cato Institute, a libertarian think tank. “We crossed the Rubicon,” Lincicome said. “The invocation of 338 is the nuclear option for Trump tariffs.” Advertisement Tariffs Are Also A Political Challenge For Trump The new tariffs carry serious political and economic risks for Trump ahead of the November midterm elections for control of Congress. His “Liberation Day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. President Donald Trump’s “Liberation Day” tariffs last year in April provoked a financial market meltdown over concerns about inflation and a recession, prompting him to walk back the rates for a period of negotiation. (AP Photo/Mark Schiefelbein, File) AP Photo/Mark Schiefelbein The Supreme Court ruled this February that Trump had lacked the legal authority to impose the tariffs by declaring an economic emergency, causing the administration to find alternative ways to raise import taxes based on a series of legal authorities. Advertisement Tariffs are taxes on imports, which companies can then pass along to consumers in the form of higher prices. The president maintains that the costs created by tariffs will cause manufacturing to relocate to the U.S., though there is little evidence of that in the economic data. “These new taxes will raise prices on American families and likely lead to retaliation against the very industries Trump purportedly wants to protect,” said Rep. Suzan DelBene, D-Wash., who is chair of the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee. The latest import taxes could worsen Trump’s weak ratings on the economy. He promised voters when running for the presidency that he would bring prices down, but the annual inflation rate has risen since he became president because the tariffs and the war in Iran are pushing up oil prices. Advertisement Trump Has Repeatedly Targeted Canada On Trade Issues The Trump administration official said the president had also requested that his aides look into additional tariffs on Canada because its wildfires hurt air quality in the U.S. He had publicly threatened to do so in social media posts. At the World Cup final on Sunday, Trump watched the game with Carney. The Trump administration official said their time together at the game was not a working visit to discuss trade and tariffs. Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after the U.S. president imposed tariffs on Canada under the pretext that it should do more to stop fentanyl smuggling. Advertisement Trump noted in his autos proclamation that Canada maintained, starting in April 2025, a 25% tariff on the imports of U.S. motor vehicles that did not qualify for preferential treatment under the USMCA. The White House said that, regarding alcohol, all but two Canadian provinces and territories halted the purchase and retailing of American alcoholic beverages beginning last year, which was also a response to Trump’s tariffs and taunts of making Canada the 51st state. But Trump has long objected to Canada’s treatment of U.S. cheese, saying in his proclamation that Canada discriminates against the U.S. compared to Europe on dairy products. Advertisement Trump and Carney have had a frosty relationship, with Carney, a former central banker, who pledged to go “elbows up” for Canada during his election campaign last year. At the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, in January, Carney called out Trump — without naming him — by saying that the “most powerful” countries are using the economy to coerce less powerful nations. Trump responded at the time by saying: “Canada lives because of the United States.” ___ Associated Press writer Jim Morris contributed from Vancouver, British Columbia.

topic: Zelensky Ukraines Protests Dismisses General Sacks

left-center.title: Zelensky Dismisses Ukraine’s Top General After Protests

center.title: Zelensky sacks Ukraine's top army commander after days of protests

farLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerLeftText: No article found in this category for this topic.
centerText:
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky has dismissed the country's army commander-in-chief, Oleksandr Syrskyi.

It comes just days after the removal of popular Defence Minister Mykhailo Fedorov - a move that triggered street protests across the country.

Initial rumours that the end of Fedorov's tenure was related to a conflict between him and Syrskyi were soon confirmed.

Fedorov, 35, has been widely praised for energising the ministry and transforming the armed forces, as well as leading a drive against corruption. Syrskyi, 60, is seen by many in Ukraine as a Soviet-school commander reluctant to implement radical changes in the army.

He was replaced by Mykhailo Drapatyi, 43, who is a popular, battle-hardened general.

"I will work responsibly, with full focus, and with respect for the people who are defending our country today," Drapatyi said on Facebook.

He also thanked Syrskyi "for his consistent work to strengthen the Ukrainian army," adding: "I grew up in it."

In a video post on social media late on Tuesday, Zelensky thanked Syrskyi for his military campaigns, including his role in successfully defending the capital Kyiv during the first days of Russia's full-scale invasion in 2022.

Zelensky also revealed that earlier in the day, he met Fedorov and offered him a "worthy position in power", but did not say whether the offer had been accepted.

Fedorov was last week replaced by Yevhenii Khmara, a former top official at Ukraine's Security Service (SBU).

"All decisions will be formalised tomorrow," Zelensky added in his post.

farLeftText: From 3h ago 19.14 EDT Trump announces still more tariffs, this time on generic drugs Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028. The announcement, in a social media post, came just three months after the president issued a proclamation saying that generic pharmaceuticals “are not subject to tariffs at this time” but that decision “will be reassessed in one year.” Trump’s latest head-spinning reversal on tariff policy means, he said, that generic drugs will continue to be imported tariff-free until 1 August 2028, when a 100% tariff would be imposed “for a one year period of time, and 200% thereafter.” The goal of the policy, Trump said, was to coerce drug makers into building plants in the United States. Trump did not state what legal authority he was claiming to impose future tariffs on generic drugs, particularly the projected 200% tariff which would come into effect in August 2029, six months after the end of his final term. Share 2m ago 21.57 EDT Closing summary This brings our live coverage of the second Trump administration to an end for the day, with just 913 more to come. Here are the latest developments: As he wages war on Iran to prevent that country from enriching uranium, Donald Trump has reportedly approved a deal with ⁠Saudi Arabia to have US firms build a civilian nuclear energy program in the kingdom, which could include a uranium enrichment facility, US officials told the Wall Street Journal. The deal, which the administration plans to submit ⁠to Congress ⁠in the ​coming days, does not include safeguards to stop ‌materials being used in nuclear weapons programs, Reuters reports. The US-Israeli war on Iran has cost the Pentagon $37.5bn so far, defense secretary Pete Hegseth told the Senate Appropriations Committee on Tuesday. “Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” New York mayor Zohran Mamdani said in a video statement in which he called the International Criminal Court warrant for the Israeli prime minister’s arrest justified but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it. Donald Trump announced a new package of tariffs on Tuesday, this time on generic drugs made overseas, to begin in 2028. The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. The Trump administration paused more than $1bn in Medicaid funding to California and Minnesota, two states run by elected Democrats, citing “suspected fraud and noncompliance”. The deal, which the administration plans to submit ⁠to Congress ⁠in the ​coming days, does not include safeguards to prevent the uranium from being used to make nuclear weapons, Reuters reports. The 30-year deal is estimated to be worth tens of billions of dollars, the Journal reports. The ​two ‌men signed a ​preliminary ​agreement in Riyadh last year. Trump has previously scoffed at the idea that Iran might need or want to enrich uranium for peaceful civilian purposes, including energy. Share Updated at 21.41 EDT 2h ago 20.22 EDT Mamdani says New York City does not have the legal authority to arrest Netanyahu for war crimes, but calls on federal government to do so “Benjamin Netanyahu is a war criminal, the architect of a horrific genocide against the Palestinian people,” New York mayor Zohran Mamdani said on Tuesday in a video statement in which he called the International Criminal Court warrant for the Israeli prime minister’s arrest justified but admitted that he lacks the legal authority to enforce it. “My administration has reviewed every avenue available under applicable law to determine whether New York City could execute the International Criminal Court’s arrest warrant if Benjamin Netanyahu came here,” Mamdani said. “It is clear that we do not have the independent legal authority to enforce this warrant.” “The federal government, however, does— and I call on them to join the ICC and execute this warrant,” the mayor added, one day after Donald Trump rejected the idea, writing on social media: “Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while in the United States of America.” “Benjamin Netanyahu is not welcome in New York City, nor is any other war criminal at large,” Mamdani said. A video statement from New York City’s mayor, Zohran Mamdani, released on Tuesday. Netanyahu is expected to travel to New York in September to address the United Nations general assembly. For many years, the Israeli prime minister has made the supposed threat posed by Iran’s nuclear enrichment program the focus of his annual speech to the UN. Last year, scores of delegates walked out of the hall before he could begin. Unlike 125 other countries, the United States is not a state party to the Rome Statute, which established the ICC, and so is not obligated to arrest indicted war crimes suspects. Last week, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, announced “a sweeping campaign to dismantle the threat posed by the International Criminal Court to U.S. sovereignty” and promised to “systematically disable the ICC’s ability to operate”. The arrest of accused war crimes suspects has tested notions of sovereignty in international law in the past. In 1998, the former Chilean dictator Augusto Pinochet was arrested while visiting London on the orders of the Spanish judge Baltasar Garzón who sought Pinochet’s extradi­tion to Spain to face charges of genocide, terrorism and torture. Pinochet was was detained for 16 months before the UK’s home secretary decide that he was too ill to stand trial and allowed him to return home on compassionate grounds. In 1960, Adolf Eichmann, the Nazi official who organized the deportation of millions of European Jews to death camps, was secretly arrested by Israeli commandos in Argentina, where he had been living under an assumed name. Rather than request his extradition from an Argentine government that had given refuge to prominent Nazis, the Israeli snatch team held Eichmann for 10 days and smuggled him out of the country disguised as an El Al flight attendant to stand trial in Jerusalem. Share Updated at 21.24 EDT 2h ago 19.44 EDT Democrats say they will repeal Trump's tariffs As Donald Trump announces new tariffs daily, leading one sober trade expert to observe, “Tariff Crazytown is officially BACK”, Democratic lawmakers are lining up to promise to repeal the Trump tariffs as soon as they regain control of Congress. “Democrats will flip the House. And on Day 1 of next term, Democrats will introduce legislation to repeal trump’s disastrous tariffs. And we will pass it,” congressman Ted Lieu, a California Democrat, wrote after Trump imposed 50% tariffs on a range of goods from Canada on Monday, citing Section 338 of the disastrous Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930 as his legal authority. “Donald Trump has abused every trade authority at his disposal, and he is now excavating a law from the Great Depression to once again impose vast, unilateral tariffs on products from one of our closest allies and trading partners,” senator Ron Wyden, an Oregon Democrat, said in a statement. “This is yet another shakedown that will raise the cost-of-living for Americans, their families, and small businesses across the country. Trump cannot be trusted with tariff authority, and I will be introducing a bill very soon to rein him in and put Congress back in the driver’s seat.” In a Substack column republished by the Toronto Star on Tuesday, the Nobel-winning economist Paul Krugman wrote: double quotation mark The Trade Act of 1930, generally known as the Smoot-Hawley tariff, lost its standing as the worst trade policy action in US history when Donald Trump imposed his “Liberation Day” tariffs in April 2025. But Smoot-Hawley was widely recognized as a terrible mistake soon after its passage, as it provoked widespread retaliation and contributed to the downward spiral of world trade that accompanied and reinforced the Great Depression. … Yesterday the Trump administration invoked an obscure and never-before-used provision of Smoot-Hawley — Section 338 — to impose 50 percent tariffs on a wide range of Canadian goods. … Whatever the real motivation for these new tariffs, they are almost surely illegal. They are definitely a violation of the U.S.-Mexico-Canada trade agreement, a revision of NAFTA negotiated and signed by Trump himself during his first term … So these latest actions confirm what most people around the world, from the European Commission to Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard, have already figured out: a deal with Donald Trump lasts only until he feels like breaking it. At the Senate hearing, which has now concluded, senator Dick Durbin, an Illinois Democrat, asked Hegseth to explain why the Pentagon was asking Congress for an additional $67bn to pay for the war Trump started on 28 February when “the Department of Defense still has $75bn in unobligated funding from last year’s reconciliation package”. US defense secretary Pete Hegseth said that the US war on Iran had cost the Pentagon $37.5 “That 75,” Hegseth replied, “there’s plans and designs on that part of the rebuild of our military which had to be undertaken.” Hegseth then launched into a deeply partisan attack on his predecessor, Lloyd Austin, the retired army general, for supposedly pursuing “woke priorities” instead of military readiness. “I don’t know what Lloyd Austin did for four years, I really don’t,” he went on. “The amount of neglect, the amount of stale thinking and bureaucracy proceed staggering when we took over.” “Let me interrupt your speech for a minute, Mr Secretary,” Durbin cut in. “You estimated at one point that the cost of the war in Iran was $29bn, but then added a caveat that there was a replacement of munitions, for example. Do you have a new estimate about what the war has cost us so far in this the fifth month of the first year of this war in Iran?” the senator asked. “Senator, the estimate we have, as of today, is $37.5bn,” Hegseth answered. Pressed further by Durbin, Hegseth said that was the estimated cost of the war on Iran through the end of the US government’s financial year on 30 September. Share Updated at 18.35 EDT 4h ago 17.59 EDT Voting rights expert calls New Jersey voter registration glitch 'miniscule' The White House called on Tuesday for the House and Senate to pass the Republican SAVE America Act, which would require proof of citizenship to register to vote, and photo ID to cast a ballot, after New Jersey’s governor, Mikie Sherrill, revealed that a computer software error in the state’s automatic voter registration system for licensed drivers mistakenly registered about 6,600 people who identified themselves as non-US citizens in 2023 and 2024. According to Sherrill, who was not in office at the time, “fewer than 400 individuals who were newly registered for the first time because of the error voted. They were registered as Democrats, Republicans and Unaffiliated voters and were scattered across the state.” David Becker, a voting rights expert who leads the nonprofit Center for Election Innovation & Research, put the size of the glitch into context in a statement sent to reporters. “While administrative errors such as New Jersey’s do infrequently occur, they are very rare and affect a very small fraction of voters. The states entrusted to run our elections are best positioned to detect and correct these errors, as they have access to substantially better data than the federal government,” Becker said. “As CEIR’s own research affirms, noncitizen registration remains extremely rare, and noncitizen voting is infinitesimally rare. In this case, the number of wrongful registrations represents a miniscule fraction, roughly 0.1%, of New Jersey’s total active voter registration records in 2024,” he continued. “The number of potential ballots cast from those incorrect registrations would represent roughly 0.009% of all ballots cast in New Jersey’s 2024 general election, when Kamala Harris defeated President Trump by more than 250,000 votes,” Becker added. “Joe Biden actually defeated Trump by a margin three times as large before this error took place.” Share Updated at 18.23 EDT 5h ago 17.09 EDT Hegseth refuses to say that US military has ability to destroy Iranian facility buried beneath Pickaxe mountain At the ongoing Senate Appropriations Committee hearing, senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, pressed US defense secretary Pete Hegseth to say whether the US military could bomb a fortified underground site linked to Iran’s nuclear program known as Pickaxe mountain, which the US has not yet targeted. “We need straight answers,” Kennedy said with some exasperation. “Do we have the ability to destroy whatever is underneath Pickaxe mountain?” “Senator, a lot of our capabilities are classified, but if anybody in the world can reach anything on God’s given, God’s green Earth, it’s the United States military,” Hegseth replied. Kennedy’s question was perhaps triggered by reports that experts suggest the site is beyond ​the reach of the most powerful bunker ⁠buster bombs in ​the US arsenal. Donald Trump was asked by a reporter earlier on Tuesday if he believes an Israeli intelligence assessment that Iran has moved nuclear centrifuges into tunnels deep inside the mountain, called Kolang Gaz La in Persian, which is about a mile from Iran’s badly damaged ⁠Natanz uranium enrichment ​facility. “I think that they may have; we don’t have it on record,” Trump replied, before veering into a digression to cast the speculation about the mountain as possibly flawed reporting from news outlets. “All we do is read the fake news, you know, we see… the news is so wrong so many times, so you know, maybe this one they have right.” “It doesn’t mean anything unless they have the material. You’re talking about the centrifuges,” Trump added. “We follow the material, that’s where the action is, and we’ll be hitting that area very, probably pretty soon and very heavily.” According to the nonpartisan Institute for Science and International Security think tank in Washington, this site was “not visibly attacked” by US bombers in 2025, when US bombers dropped two two GBU-57 bunker buster bombs on the nearby nuclear facility at Natanz. “Iran announced in 2020 that the purpose of the Pickaxe tunnel complex is to hold an advanced centrifuge assembly facility that can produce several thousand centrifuges a year,” the institute reported last year. “In addition, based on the estimated size of the underground halls, the Institute several years ago assessed that site could also hold a centrifuge enrichment facility.” Share 5h ago 16.57 EDT Chris Stein The Senate intelligence committee on Tuesday advanced the nomination of Jay Clayton to serve as the US intelligence director, despite criticism from Democrats. Donald Trump had nominated Clayton, now the top federal prosecutor in the southern district of New York, for the role of the director of national intelligence (DNI) last month after the departure of Tulsi Gabbard. Intelligence committee members questioned Clayton at a hearing last week, and its Republican chair, Tom Cotton, announced that it had reported his nomination favorably by a 9-8 vote, teeing him up for a future vote on his confirmation by the full Senate. “Jay Clayton is a highly qualified nominee with a deep experience combatting a wide range of national security threats,” Cotton said in a statement. “I’m pleased that the Senate Intelligence Committee voted to advance his nomination to be Director of National Intelligence,” he added. Cotton did not provide a breakdown of how lawmakers voted. The committee is composed of nine Republicans and eight Democrats, and a spokesperson for the Democratic vice-chair, Mark Warner, said he had opposed the nomination. At his hearing last week, members of the Democratic minority criticized Clayton, a former Wall Street attorney who served as chair of the Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC), for refusing to say that Joe Biden won the 2020 election. He also faced questions about previous statements he made echoing Trump’s claim that US elections have been marred by fraud, and concerns about politicization at the office of the DNI, which serves to coordinate the nation’s intelligence agencies. topic: Storm Tropical Bertha Expected Weaken Moves

left.title: Tropical Storm Bertha expected to weaken as it moves toward US

left-center.title: Tropical Storm Bertha closes in on Gulf Coast with up to 4 ft of storm surge expected

farLeftText:
Tropical Storm Bertha strengthened in the Gulf of Mexico on Tuesday but was expected to weaken this week as it chugs closer to the US, potentially bringing coastal flooding and heavy rain from the Florida Panhandle to Louisiana.

Those areas along the northern Gulf coast were under a tropical storm warning through Wednesday and could see rain and flooding into Thursday morning, the Miami-based National Hurricane Center said. New Orleans also was under a tropical storm warning.

The storm was about 120 miles (190km) south-west of Panama City, Florida, and 105 miles (170km) south-east of Mobile, Alabama, the Miami-based National Hurricane Center (NHC) said on Tuesday evening. It was moving at 6 mph (9 km/h) to the west with maximum sustained winds near 60 mph (95 km/h), the hurricane center said. Gradual weakening was forecast Wednesday through Friday.

Bertha was forecast to make landfall Wednesday night somewhere in the coastal and lightly populated part of Louisiana south-east of New Orleans, said Michael Buchanan, a meteorologist with the National Weather Service in Louisiana.

Wind will be the biggest threat there, he said.

Bertha is the second tropical storm of the Atlantic hurricane season after Tropical Storm Arthur brought heavy rainfall to the south-eastern US in June.

The storm was expected to stay near or along the northern Gulf coast, dropping heavy rain on coastal areas of Florida, Alabama, Mississippi and Louisiana while pushing west toward Texas.

Potentially life-threatening storm surges of up to 4ft (1.2 meters) are possible in portions of Louisiana, Alabama and Florida, the NHC said, warning Bertha could spin off an isolated tornado or two over the Florida Big Bend region.

Areas of flash flooding also were possible through Friday along the coast from western Florida to middle Texas, forecasters said, adding that rainfall could total 3in to 5in (7cm to 12cm) along the Louisiana coastline, with higher amounts in some scattered areas.

Red flags on the beach at Orange Beach, Alabama, cautioned swimmers about high surf and currents. In Escambia county, Florida, emergency officials offered sand for people living in flood-prone areas to use for sandbags.

Meanwhile, in the eastern Pacific Ocean, Hurricane Fausto churned far from Mexico’s Baja California peninsula and posed no threat to land, the NHC said.

Fausto gained hurricane status on Monday night. It was about 780 miles (1,260 km) south-west of the southern tip of the Baja California peninsula, had top sustained winds of 75mph (120 km/h) and was moving north-west at 12mph (19 km/h).

The storm was expected to strengthen on Tuesday and Wednesday. No coastal watches or warnings were in effect, though ocean swells generated by the storm could cause life-threatening surf and rip tide conditions on the Baja California coast, the NHC said.

Guardian staff contributed reporting

left.title: Hegseth tells Senate war on Iran has so far cost $37.5bn at hearing to request more funds

center.title: Iran war has cost US $37.5bn so far, Hegseth says, as Pentagon seeks billions more

farLeftText: The US defense secretary, Pete Hegseth told the Senate on Tuesday that the US-Israel war on Iran had cost $37.5bn so far, as he faced lawmakers furious over the conflict’s soaring price tag and the deaths last week of three American service members. During an appearance before the Senate appropriations committee, Hegseth, whose opening remarks were repeatedly interrupted by protesters, provided an updated account of the war’s cost – up from previous estimates – and defended the Trump administration’s roughly $88bn supplemental funding request, calling it “urgent, necessary”. Alongside the chair of the joint chiefs, Gen Dan Caine, Hegseth argued that the Pentagon was running low on cash to keep the fight going. Of the total request, as much as $67.1bn is earmarked specifically to cover the costs of the Iran war effort. “Without these funds, we face critical shortfalls,” Hegseth told the Senate panel, warning that it would threaten the defense department’s ability to “pay our service members, rapidly replenish equipment and munitions, and sustain vital operations without disruption”. It was the defense secretary’s first sustained public questioning since a brief ceasefire collapsed and the war roared back to life. At least two US soldiers were killed in an Iranian attack on Friday, and another died in Iraq destroying an Iranian drone, pushing the American death toll since February to 17. Nearly 100 troops have been wounded in the conflict, officials confirm, though most injuries were minor. Patty Murray, the committee’s Democratic vice-chair, demanded Hegseth explain what the war had actually achieved, accusing the administration of shifting its rationale by the week and warning the conflict risked becoming “another forever war”. “We hear a different rationale for this war every few days,” Murray said in her opening remarks, noting the justification had moved from pre-empting threats that “did not exist” to regime change, then to dismantling Iran’s nuclear programme and navy, and now, she said, to simply reopening the strait of Hormuz, a waterway that was “fully open, toll-free, before this war began”. “Here we are, five months later”, she continued. “The strait is closed. The ceasefire hasn’t just disintegrated – this war is now spiraling out of control again.” Under questioning from lawmakers, Hegseth cast the funding request as the price of deterrence rather than admitting failure, and framed the Pentagon’s overhaul under the US president as a break from what he described as years of slow bureaucracy. “The best way to create peace is to prepare for war, to deter it,” Hegseth told the panel, arguing the department was “building a force so capable that it would be absurd for anyone to challenge it”. Hegseth said the $21bn readiness portion of the request would cover military pay, replace equipment used in recent operations and stabilize fuel shortfalls, while the $46bn capability portion would expand production of “solid rocket motors, Jdams [joint direct attack munitions], hypersonics and counter-drone capabilities”. Without the funds, he warned, the department faced “critical shortfalls” that threaten its ability to pay troops and replenish munitions, and he cautioned that failing to fund the Pentagon at $1.5tn was “the greatest threat that our nation faces”. Some Democrats, including Chris Coons of Delaware, plan to direct criticism at Russell Vought, director of office of management and budget, who is not testifying but who earlier told US Congress that the war’s running cost was about $30bn, a figure that is largely believed by experts to be a severe undercount. Hegseth and Caine faced potential threats from within their own party. Susan Collins of Maine, chair of the committee, and Alaska’s Lisa Murkowski, two prominent Republicans, have both crossed the aisle to back war-powers resolutions aimed at reining in the president’s authority over the conflict. Collins, who is facing a competitive re-election contest, has previously voiced frustration that the administration’s strategy for the war seems to shift almost daily. During the hearing, she asked Hegseth about the effect on military exercises if the supplemental funding is not provided. The defense secretary said that current and future training “would have to be curtailed” if the Pentagon does not get its budget needs met urgently. Senator John Kennedy, a Republican of Louisiana, pressed the panelists on the situation in the strait of Hormuz, asking if Iran will impose tolls on commercial vessels passing through the vital waterway which carried about a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. “That’s a hypothetical question,” Caine replied, “and I don’t know the answer to that.” “Guys, we need straight answers,” Kennedy shot back. Both congressional chambers have passed a war-powers resolution urging the president to seek congressional authorization to continue military operations in Iran. Asked why the administration had not yet sought congressional approval for the war, Hegseth said it was the view of his department that “standalone Article II authorities exist for us to conduct this operation on behalf of the defense of the American people”. The defense secretary evaded the lawmaker’s question about whether he would use the approval of any supplemental funding as a “substitute for explicit congressional authorization”. House representatives have separately accused the Pentagon of stonewalling on basic figures: Betty McCollum, the top Democrat on the House defense appropriations panel, said the department has missed a deadline to detail war costs and has not disclosed how many precision munitions remain after the military burned through thousands of them in Iran.

centerText:
The war with Iran has cost the US some $37.5bn (£28bn) so far - an increase of nearly $8bn since the last estimate in May, Defence Secretary Pete Hegseth has said.

In his first appearance before the Senate Appropriations Committee since hostilities between the US and Iran restarted, Hegseth said that military training would have to be curtailed unless lawmakers approved an urgent funding boost.

He said he was seeking $87bn in congressional funding for his department, with $67bn of that destined for operations in the Middle East.

The hearing was interrupted several times by anti-war protesters, with some shouting "stop bombing our children in Iran and Palestine".

Hegseth's appearance before the committee came at a pivotal time in the war after three US soldiers were killed in the Middle East.

The White House asked Congress in April for $1.5tn for the Pentagon over the next fiscal year, which would raise American military spending to an all-time high in the modern era.

left.title: Middle East crisis live: tankers carrying Saudi oil turned back after Houthi threats; Trump claims US will hit Iran nuclear storage facility ‘pretty soon’

left-center.title: What is Pickaxe Mountain? Trump says US will hit Iranian nuclear site 'pretty soon'

right.title: Trump: U.S. ‘Not Finished at All’ in Iran; Pickaxe Mountain Strike Coming ‘Pretty Soon’

farLeftText: A vessel in the strait of Hormuz, as seen from Musandam, Oman. With the war shutting the strait of Hormuz, the Red Sea has served as the main alternate route out for millions of barrels of Saudi oil per day, diverted by pipeline through Yanbu. double quotation mark The developments represent the first confirmed changes to commercial tanker routing following the (Houthi) embargo and are likely to increase disruption to Saudi crude exports and regional shipping patterns.” The announcement already appears to be having an impact. Two tankers which had just loaded Saudi crude bound for China and India at Saudi Arabia’s Red Sea port of Yanbu made U-turns on Tuesday, heading towards the Suez Canal rather ‌than out through the Bab el-Mandeb into the Indian Ocean. To recap, the Houthis , who control northern and western Yemen including the coast at the mouth of the Red Sea, announced a naval blockade on Saudi Arabia on ⁠Monday. Israel has 'no interest' in joining US strikes against Iran, says Smotrich Trump says US will help Lebanon during meeting with Aoun The confirmation brings the death toll to 18 US service members killed since the war began . The US military has confirmed a third service member is believed to be dead in Friday’s attack on a base in Jordan and identified them as Sgt. Angel S. Rampersad, 28, of New York . Donald Trump has returned to threatening Iranian nuclear sites, in particular Pickaxe Mountain. Here’s an explainer on the mountain, including why it has captured Trump’s attention and how the US could potentially strike at the deeply buried target. 45m ago 21.24 EDT Global trade could be disrupted by Houthi rebels at a second chokepoint Coming back now to the blockade of Saudi Arabia by Yemen’s Houthi rebels, and how it could affect oil and trade. The rebels say they have closed the Bab el-Mandeb strait to Saudi-linked shipping in retaliation for the kingdom’s blockade on Yemen and a recent attack on the international airport in Yemen’s rebel-held capital, Sanaa. The Houthis claim to have forced six ships to reroute on Tuesday, but there was no independent confirmation. “If Bab el-Mandeb comes under pressure at the same time as shipping in the Gulf, you have disruption around two of the world’s most important maritime routes at once,” says Clionadh Raleigh, the founder of ACLED, a conflict monitoring service. More than 7 million barrels of petroleum a day transited Bab el-Mandeb in June, compared to around 4 million before the Iran war, according to an analysis published on Monday by Allison Minor, the director of the Atlantic Council’s Project for Middle East Integration. View image in fullscreen Houthi military spokesman Yahya Sarea delivers a televised statement on 20 July. Photograph: Yahya Arhab/EPA Saudi Arabia has been recently exporting some 4 million barrels of oil a day through its Red Sea port of Yanbu, blunting the impact of the closure of the strait of Hormuz. Saudi could still get crude to market without using Bab el-Mandeb, by shipping up to 2.5 million barrels a day across the Red Sea to Egypt, whose SUMED pipeline can carry it to the Mediterranean. It could also ship 1 million barrels a day through the Suez Canal. International shippers could also reroute, as they did at the height of the war in Gaza. But that means going the long way around the Cape of Good Hope off South Africa, adding one or two weeks to voyages – and raising costs.
centerLeftText: Pickaxe Mountain, under construction, has been spared in past attacks. What is Pickaxe Mountain? Trump says US will hit Iranian nuclear site 'pretty soon' A satellite view shows tunnel entrances at Pickaxe Mountain, of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 30, 2026. A satellite view shows tunnel entrances at Pickaxe Mountain, of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 30, 2026. A satellite view shows tunnel entrances at Pickaxe Mountain, of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 30, 2026. A satellite view shows tunnel entrances at Pickaxe Mountain, of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 30, 2026. President Donald Trump said Tuesday the U.S. would target Pickaxe Mountain, an Iranian nuclear site that is under construction and buried deep below the ground, as the war escalates amid 10 straight days of strikes. "We'll be hitting that area pretty soon, and very heavily," Trump told reporters in the Oval Office, repeating a threat he's made against the site in recent weeks. The Wall Street Journal reported that Israeli intelligence assessed Iran transferred centrifuges, which could be used to enrich uranium, the essential process in building a nuclear weapon, to Pickaxe Mountain last fall. The facility has been under construction for years and is only miles away from Natanz, one of three nuclear site targeted by U.S. and Israeli airstrikes in a June 2025 operation. President Donald Trump meets with Lebanese President Joseph Aoun in the Oval Office at the White House in Washington, July 21, 2026. Mark Schiefelbein/AP Photo Trump did not deny the Wall Street Journal report when asked by a reporter. The president said Iran "may have" moved the centrifuges there, but "we don't have it on record." "Maybe this one they have right," Trump said about the newspaper. "But it's okay. It doesn't mean anything unless they have the material." "We follow the material," Trump said, referring to the uranium itself. Iran is believed to possess close to 1,000 pounds of highly-enriched uranium, which is near weapons-grade and which Trump has said the U.S. would secure in order to ensure the military campaign was successful in inhibiting Iran's progress toward a nuclear weapon. Separate from the discussion around Pickaxe, Trump said there was evidence Iran was looking to rebuild its nuclear program, which was severely damaged in U.S. strikes last summer but apparently spared so far in the war in Iran, which began in February. "They're trying to possibly reconstitute a site," Trump said Tuesday. "We'll hit that site. Any site where they're even thinking about nuclear, we'll be hitting it very, very powerfully." Reporting from the Wall Street Journal said it was unclear whether the transfer of centrifuges to Pickaxe represented an effort to build an enrichment site or was merely done to protect the equipment from destruction. What is Pickaxe Mountain? Known as Kūh-e Kolang or "Mount Kolang" in Iran, the complex includes a series of tunnels and facilities built into the mountain. It was declared in 2021 by Iran to be a centrifuge assembly site after a similar plant in neighboring Natanz was destroyed. Analysts at the Institute for Science and International Security (ISIS), who reviewed recent satellite imagery, say that construction is still underway at the site. They add that it is buried 100 meters under the ground, and that it's unclear when it could be operational. Kelsey Davenport, the director for nonproliferation policy at the Arms Control Association, told ABC News that "the location of the site does pose concern." Inspectors from the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), the United Nations' nuclear watchdog, have been barred from entering Iran since the U.S. attacked the country earlier this year, leaving profound question marks about Iran's nuclear capabilities. A satellite view shows tunnel entrances at Pickaxe Mountain, of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 30, 2026. Vantor via Reuters Analysts say it's unlikely the U.S. would have spared Pickaxe in the past if it believed it posed a threat. "I would say it's notable that the U.S. and Israel did not even attempt to target the facility in June 2025, or again after attacking Iran this year," Davenport said. "Given the intensity of the U.S. and Israeli intelligence penetration of Iran's nuclear program, I think that suggests that the U.S. does not view Pickaxe as an imminent threat." Yet, the presence of centrifuges or other nuclear technologies could motivate a U.S. attack, experts said. Some analysts said a U.S. attack could emulate its strikes against Isfahan in 2025, since the depth of Pickaxe would make direct targeting difficult -- even with the military's most powerful bomb, a massive ordnance penetrator, or MOP. The U.S. could conceivably use the MOPs to target Pickaxe, but there would be "no guarantee that the site would be destroyed," Davenport said. ISIS assesses that "the site, in its present condition, would be more suitable for ground forces to attack or sabotage." Still, the bombs could exploit vulnerabilities at Pickaxe. Collapsing the entrances could complicate construction efforts which are underway there, and an attempt to dig out the entrances would be visible via satellites, experts said, providing a kind of "early warning system" for efforts to rebuild. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, asked whether the U.S. could destroy whatever is below Pickaxe during a hearing on Capitol Hill on Tuesday, said that "if anybody in the world can reach anything on ... God's green earth, it's the United States military, the most powerful military in the world." Uncertainty about centrifuges Little is known about the whereabouts or status of Iranian centrifuges. Iran has not complied with its obligations under international law to allow the IAEA to access centrifuge manufacturing sites since 2020. Before the war, Secretary of State Marco Rubio said that the U.S. assessed that Iran was "not enriching" its nuclear material, a process involving centrifuges, at the time. Olli Heinonen, a former deputy director-general for safeguards at the IAEA, said Iran has developed a more sophisticated program with "more independence using their own industry" in the years since the international community began negotiating with Iran over its nuclear capabilities. According to Heinonen, as many as 20 facilities across Iran produced centrifuges, potentially leaving the machines dispersed around the country. A satellite view shows vehicles at an entrance to Pickaxe Mountain tunnels, of the of the Natanz nuclear facility, near Natanz, Iran, June 21, 2026. Vantor via Reuters "There is a real question mark about what types of centrifuges survived [and] how many survived" the U.S. strikes last summer, Davenport said. "And that has a direct relevance to how quickly Iran could reconstitute its program," she said. "In my opinion, these uncertainties underscore the critical necessity of the IAEA returning to Iran to resume inspections." Heinonen said components manufactured around the country is "the big question," because it's unlikely that the U.S. destroyed all 20 sites in its 2026 bombing campaign. "If they have all the components, you can assemble centrifuges somewhere else," he said.
